## Dieses Dokument

- Titel: The
                                Buried Candelabra
- Signatur: SZ-AP2/W-F4.1
- Sprache: Englisch
- Objekttyp: Typoskript
- Umfang: 20 Blatt
- Anmerkungen: Notiz „Leftwich“ auf 1. Blatt auf beiden
                                Exemplaren

pages.text:
THE BURIED CANDELABRA.
By Stefan Zweig.
On a bright June day in the year 455, in the Circus
Maximus in Rome, just as a combat between two giant Heruli and
a pack of Hyrcanian boars had ended bloodily, about the third
hour of the afternoon, a growing uneasiness began to spread among
the thousands of spectators. At first, only those who were near
the detached State box, richly decorated with carpets and images,
in which the Emperor Maximus sat among his courtiers, noticed
that a messenger had entered it, covered with dust, and obviously
just dismounted after a wild ride, and that no sooner had the
Emperor heard what he had to say than, contrary to all custom,
he rose in the midst of the exciting games; the entire Court
followed him with the same conspicuous haste, and presently the
seats reserved for the Senators and other dignitaries had also
emptied. Such precipitate departure could not be without
weighty cause. In vain the fanfares announced a new combat,
the grating was lifted and a growling black-maned Nubian lion
was driven towards the short swords of the gladiators - a dark
wave of apprehension, frothing with the pale foam of questioning
and terrified faces, had surged up irresistibly and ran from tier
to tier. People sprang to their feet, pointed to the empty
seats of the patricians, questioned and clamoured, shouted and
whistled; then suddenly a confused rumour spread, without any-
one knowing who had started it, that the Vandals, the dreaded
pirates of the Mediterranean, had landed an immense fleet at
Portus, and were already on their way against the unsuspecting

- 2 -
city.. The Vandals! At first, the word ran but as a pale
whisper from lip to lip, then suddenly it became a shrill, leaping
cry, "The Barbarians! The Barbarians!" hundred-voiced, thousand-
voiced it reverberated through the tiered stone round of the
circus, and soon as though driven by a thunderous gust of wind,
the vast mass of people were rushing in frenzied panic towards
the exit. All order collapsed. The guards, the soldiers of
the watch left their places and fled with the rest; people jumped
over the seats, they cleared a path for themselves with fists and
swords, they trampled down shrieking women and children; round
the exits shafts formed crammed with masses of swirling, shriek-
ing people, spinning round in the crush. In a few minutes the
great circus that had just held eighty thousand people jammed
together in a dark, clamouring block, had been swept entirely
clear. The tiered oval lay marble and mute and empty in the
summer sun like a deserted stone quarry. Only in the arena
below - the gladiators had long fled after the rest - stood
the forgotten lion, shaking his black mane, and roared his
challenge to the sudden void.
The Vandals! Messenger after messenger now came dash-
ing in, and each report was worse than the one before. They had
landed from hundreds of sailing ships and galleys, and nimble,
lightly-moving army; white-cloaked Berber and Numidian horsemen
already sped on swift, long-necked steeds along the streets of
Portus, in advance of the actual army. To-morrow or the day
after the hoardes of robbers would be at the gates, and there

- 3 -
were no preparations for defence. The mercenary army was fight-
ing far away, near Ravenna, and the fortified walls that Alaric
had razed still lay in ruins. No one thought of defence. The
rich and the patricians hastily got ready mules and carts to save
at least a portion of their possessions together with their lives.
But it was already too late. For the populace would not
tolerate it that the patricians should oppress them in good times
and desert like cowards in misfortune. And when the Emperor
Maximus tried to escape from his Palace with his baggage, he
was assailed first by curses and then by stones. Finally, the
exasperated rabble fell upon the coward and slew their miserable
Emperor with cudgels and hatchets in the street. It is true
that afterwards the gates were closed as every evening; but the
terror was thereby entirely shut in inside the city; a foreboding
of something terrible hung like a foul marshy mist over the mute,
lightless houses, and the dark sank down like a stifling blanket
upon the lost city, that cowered with fear and horror. But the
eternal, indifferent stars above shone lightly and unconcenred
and the moon hung his silver horn on the azure wall of heaven,
like any other night. Rome lay sleepless, with quivering nerves,
and waited for the Barbarians, like a condemned man who has
placed his head on the block waiting for the inevitable, already
descending stroke.
Slowly, surely, strategically, victoriously the Vandals
meanwhile entered the deserted streets of Rome, from the harbour.
The fair long-haired Germanic warriors marched in good order,

- 4 -
hundred after hundred, with well-drilled military step; and
restlessly, in front, flying along without stirrups, prancing
and curvetting on their splendid thoroughbreds rode the auxilia-
ries from the desert, dark-skinned and pitch-haired Numidians.
In the midst of the train rode Genseric, the King of the Vandals.
He smiled down with indolent satisfaction from the saddle upon
his marching people. The long experienced warrior knew through
his spies that there was no fear of serious resistance, that this
time they were not preparing for a decisive battle, but merely
for unmolested plundering. Indeed, no hostile warrior appeared.
Only at the Porta Pontuensis, where the finelly-levelled road
from the harbour entered the inner part of Rome, the King was
approached by Pope Leo, wearing his full pontificals, and glitter-
ingly surrounded by all his clergy, Pope Leo, the same white-
bearded aged man who had a few years before gloriously induced
the terrible Attila to spare Rome, and the heathen Hun had with
incredible humility submitted to his will. Genseric, too,
dismounted from his horse immediately he saw the majestic white-
bearded figure, and (his right leg being shorter) he limped
courteously towards him. But he neither kissed the hand that
wore the Fisherman's ring, nor piously bent his knee, for he
was an Arian heretic who considered the Pope merely a usurper
of true Christianity, and he listened with cool arrogance to the
Pope's Latin exhortation that he should spare the sacred city.
There was no need to be afraid, he answered through his interpre-
ter, they need not fear any inhumanity from him; he was himself

- 5 -
a warrior, and a Christian. He would not burn Rome with fire,
nor destroy it, though this domineering city had razed to the
ground thousands upon thousands of cities. He would in his
magnanimity spare both the possessions of the Church and the
women, and would only plunder "Sine ferro et igne", according
to the right of the stronger and the victor. But he advised -
and Genseric said this menacingly, while his equerry already
assisted him into the stirrup - that the gates of Rome should
be opened to him without further delay.
What Genseric demanded was done. No spear was raised,
no sword was drawn. In an hour all Rome belonged to the
Vandals. But the victorious pirates did not pour into the
defenceless city like an unruly horde. In closed ranks, dis-
ciplined by Genseric's stern, imperious hand, the tall, flaxen-
haired warriors marched along the Via Triumphalis, and only
occasionally stared curiously at the thousands upon thousands
of white-eyed statues whose mute lips seemed to promise loot.
Genseric himself immediately his troops had marched in, proceeded
to the Pallatinum, the deserted Imperial residence. But he
neither accepted the homage intended by the Senators, who waited
in a frightened line, nor would he let them arrange a banquet;
he scarcely deigned to glance at the gifts which the wealthy
citizens had hoped would mitigate his severity; the stern
soldier immediately bent over a map, and prepared his plan for
the swiftest yet most thorough valuation of the city. Each
district was placed under a different centurion, who was made

- 6 -
responsible for the behaviour of his men. For what now began
was no wild and undisciplined pillage, but a planned, methodical
spoliation. Next, on Genseric's order, the gates were closed,
and sentries posted, so that not a single ornament or coin in
all that vast city could escape him. Then his soldiers seized
the boats, the vehicles, the beasts of burden, and pressed
thousands of the slaves into service, so that everything
valuable harboured in Rome should be transferred with the utmost
speed to his African lair. After that the pillage began,
systematically, and with cold, silent deliberation. Skilfully
and comfortably, like a slaughtered beast being cut up by a
butcher, the living city was during these thirteen days dis-
embowelled, and the barely quivering body was torn limb from
limb. From house to house, from temple to temple, went the
troops, led by one of the Vandal nobles, and accompanied by
a scribe, and one after another they brought out everything
valuable and movable, gold and silver vessels, ornaments, coins,
jewels, amber necklaces from the Northland, furs from Transyl-
vania, Pontian malachite and Persian hammered swords. They
forced the workmen to remove the mosaics cleanly from the
walls of the Temple, and to prise up the porphyrian slabs from
the peristyles. Everything was done with premeditation,
skilfully and precisely. The bronze chariot-teams on the
Triumphal Arch were brought down carefully, with pulleys, so
they should not be damaged; and when the building had been
despoiled, they put the slaved to removing tile by tile from

- 7 -
the gilded roof of the Jupiter Capitolinus. The bronze pillars
alone, that were too immense to be loaded quickly, were ordered
by Genseric to be broken up with hammers, or sawed apart, in
order to obtain the metal. Street after street, house after
house, was carefully cleared, and as soon as the dwellings of
the living had been completely emptied, they broke into the
tumuli, the places of the dead. From the stone sarcophagi
they tore the jewelled combs from the mouldering hair of dead
princesses, and the gold ornaments from the fleshless bones;
they removed the metal mirrors, and the signet rings from the
corpses, and their rapacious hands stile even the obol that
was placed in the grave with the dead so that they could pay
the ferryman in the next world. The entire loot collected
in the various plunderings were then piled in separate heaps
in a place previously arranged. There lay the gold-winged
Nike beside a gem-studded chest that contained the bones of a
saint, and the dice-box of a great lady. Silver ingots were
heaped beside purple robes, choice glass beside coarse metal.
The scribe noted down each article on his long parchment in
stiff Nordic characters, in order to give the spoliation some
semblance of legality; Genseric himself limped with his
retinue through the throng, thouched the things with his stick,
examined the jewels, smiled and praised. He watched with
pleasure how cart after cart, boat after boat, all heavily
loaded, left the city. But no house hurned, no blood was shed.
Calmly and methodically, as the trucks in a mine ascend and

- 8 -
descend, one empty, the other full, so for thirteen days the
carts rolled from the harbour to the sea, and from the sea to
the harbour. They rolled down full, they came back empty, and
the oxen and the mules were already groaning under the burden,
for as long as people could think back, there had never been so
much loot in thirteen days as in this Vandal pillage.
For thirteen days no human voice was heard in this
thousand-housed city. No one spoke aloud. No one laughed.
The sound of stringed instruments was hushed in the houses, and
no chanting rose in the churches. One heard only the hammering,
with which things fixed were broken away from their place, the
crash of the falling stones, the creaking of the over-loaded
carts, and the grunting of the weary beasts on whom the whips
of the tormentors fell again and again. Sometimes dogs howled;
the people had forgotten in their own fear to feed them, and
sometimes the sound of the tuba echoed dully from across the
walls, at the changing of the guards. But the people in the
houses held their breath. The city that had conquered the
world had fallen, and when the wind went at night through the
empty streets, it sounded like the feeble groaning of a wounded
man who feels the last blood draining from his veins.
On that thirteenth evening of the pillage, the Jews of
Rome sat together in Moses Abtalion's house on the left bank of
the Tiber, where the yellow stream coils lazily like an overfed
snake. He was not one of the great men in the community, nor

- 9 -
was he learned in Holy Script; he was only a hard old worker,
but they had chosen his house for their gathering because the
workshop on the ground level was more spacious than the cramped
attics of the rest. For thirteen days they had sat like that
each day, all of them together, with grey, weary faces, in their
white shrouds, and prayed with a dull, almost insensible per-
severance, in the shadows cast by the closed shutters, among the
hanging rolls, the bleached cloths, and the wide mouthed vats.
Till now they had not suffered any ill at the hands of the
Vandals. Two or three times, troops led by nobles and scribes
had passed through the narrow, low-lying street of the Jews,
where the damp from many inundations sat like fungus in the
floors of the dwelling houses, and ran in cold tears from the
dripping walls; a contemptuous glance was enough for the
experienced bandits to recognise that there was nothing in this
misery to loot. There were no marble-paved peristyles here,
no triclinium glittering with gold, no bronze statues and vases
hidden away. So the looters passed on with indifference, and
there was no danger of fire or plunder. Nevertheless, the
hearts of the Jews of Rome were oppressed, and they crowded
together in fearful foreboding. For misfortune to the city,
to the land in which they dwelt - this they knew from genera-
tion to generation - always turned out in the end to be a
misfortune for them. When times were good, the peoples forgot
about them, took no heed of them. The princes adorned them-
selves, gave themselves up to building and pomp, and the mob

- 10 -
had its cruder amusements, wild-beast baiting, hunting, and the
games. But whenever trouble came, the Jews were blamed. It
was bad if the enemy won, bad if a town was looted, bad if
pestilence of disease invaded the countries. All the evil of
the world, they knew, would turn out to be an evil for them, and
they knew, too, that there was no appeal against their fate, for
everywhere in all places they were few, everywhere and in all
places they were weak and without power. Their only weapon was
prayer.
So the Jews of Rome prayed each evening till late at night,
all these dark and perilous days of pillage. For what else
could the righteous do in an unrighteous and brutal world, where
violence always wins, than turn from earth to God? It had
been going on like this for years. They came now from the
South, now from the East and West, fair-haired and dark, alien
nations, and all of them robbers, and no sooner had one army
conquered, than another fell upon it. Everywhere on earth
the godless were at war, and would not leave the pious in peace.
So they had taken Jerusalem, Babylon and Alexandria, and to-day
it was Rome. Wherever one wished to rest, there was unrest,
wherever one sought peace, there was war; one could not escape
fate. In this disturbed world prayer alone was refuge, rest
and consolation. For prayer is wonderful. It blunts fear,
with great promise, it lulls the fears of the soul to sleep
with a singing litany, it lifts up heaviness of heart to God
upon its murmuring wings; therefore it is good to pray in

- 11 -
adversity, and better still to pray in company, for everything
that is hard becomes easier when it is borne in common, and all
good is better before God, when it is done in communion.
So the Jews of Rome sat together and prayed. The pious
murmuring flowed from their beards softly and constantly, like
the lapping of the Tiber outside their windows, quietly and
persistently scouring the planks of the river banks, and washing
the shore with its gentle motion. None of the men looked at
the others, yet their old, decrepit shoulders swayed to the same
rhythm, as they chanted the same psalms again and again, that
they had prayed a hundred, a thousand times before, and their
fathers before them and their fathers' forefathers. The lips
scarcely knew that they spoke, nor the senses what they felt;
this plaintive sound flowed on as if out of a dark enchanted
dream.
Suddenly they started up, and with one jerk the bent backs
straightened. The knocker outside had been banged vigorously
against the door. And always, it was already in their blood,
these Jews in alien places were terror-stricken by anything
sudden. For what good could come of a door opening at night?
The muttering stopped, as if cut with a scissors, and in the
silence the unconcerned flow of the river was heard more clearly.
They all listened with throats contracted. The knocker was
banged down again and a fist beat impatiently at the outer door.
"I am coming," said Abtalion, as if speaking to himself, and
shuffled out. The wax candle stuck on the table bent its

- 12 -
flame under the sharp gust of air that came from the opened door;
like inwardly the hearts of all these people, the flame trembled
suddenly and violently.
The terrified people recovered their breath when they saw
who had entered. It was Hyrcanos Ben Hillel, the Master of the
Imperial Mint, the pride of the community, for he was the only
Jew who was admitted to the Imperial Palace. He was permitted
by special favour of the Court to live on the other side of
Trastevere, and he might even wear fine coloured robes; but
now his cloak was torn, and his face was grimy.
They all crowded round him - for they had a premonition
that he brought tidings - impatient to hear what it was, and
yet troubled because they felt it was something bad.
Hyrcanos Ben Hillel drew a deep breath. They saw that
a word had stuck in his throat, and he could not get it out.
Then at last, he groaned:
"It is all over! They have got it! They have found
it!"
"Found what? Found whom?" The cry came from all of
them like one cry.
"The candelabra, the Menorah! When the Barbarians came,
I hid it in the kitchen refuse. I deliberately left the other
sacred objects in the Treasury. The Table with the Shew Bread,
the silver trumpets, Aaron's Rod, and the censers for incense;
for there are too many who know of our treasures for me to have
been able to secrete them all. I wanted to save only one of

- 13 -
the vessels of the Temple, Moses's candelabra, the candelabra
from Solomon's House, the Menorah. They had already taken
everything, the room was already bare; they were not seeking for
anything more, and I felt sure in my heart that we had saved at
least this one of the holy emblems for ourselves. But one of
the slaves, may his soul rot, had seem me hide the candelabra,
and he betrayed it to the robbers to purchase his own freedom.
He revealed to them the spot, and they dug it up. Now everything
has been taken that stood once in the Holy of Holies, in Solomon's
House, the table and the vessels, and the mitre of the Priest,
and the Menorah. To-night, this very night the Vandals carry off
the Menorah to their ships."
For a moment all were silent. Then cry after cry broke
from their pale lips:
"The Menorah... Woe, once again... The Menorah...
God's Menorah... Woe, oh woe... The Menorah from the table of
the Lord... The Menorah!"
The Jews stumbled against each other like drunken men,
they beat their breasts with their fists, they held their hips,
crying out as if they burned with pain; the sober old men raved
as if they had been suddenly struck blind.
"Silence!" commanded a powerful voice, and immediately
they were all silent. For it was the leader of the community,
the oldest, the wisest among them who ordered to be silent, the
great interpreter of the Scriptures, Rabbi Eleazar, whom they
called Kab ve Nake, the pure and clear. He was nearly eighty
years old, and his beard was snow-white around his face, his

- 14 -
forehead was furrowed with the painful ploughshare of relentless
thought; but the eye beneath the bushy brows had remained like
a star, kind and clear. He raised his hand, small and yellow
and wrinkled like the many parchments he had written upon, and
he cut the air with it in a straight line, as though he wished
to push aside the tumult like an unpleasant smoke, and create
a clear place for his considered words.
"Silence!" he repeated. "Children cry out when they are
afraid. Men think. Sit down, all of you, and let us take
counsel. The spirit is more active when the body rests."
The men seated themselves shamefacedly on stools and
benches. Rabbi Eleazar spoke softly, as if he were consulting
with himself.
"It is a calamity, a great calamity. Long ago they
were taken from us, the holy vessels, and none of us was ever
allowed to look upon them in the Emperor's chamber, save this one
man alone, Hyrcanos Ben Hillel. Yet we knew that they were
hidden away since the time of Titus. They were still here, and
near to us. Alien Rome seemed more friendly to us when we
remembered that the sacred vessels that had wandered for a
thousand years, that had been in Jerusalem and Babylon, and had
always returned home, now rested together with us in the same
city. We were not allowed to lay bread on the sacred table,
yet whenever we broke bread we had this table in mind. We might
not place a candle in the sacred candelabra, but whenever we
lighted a candle we remembered the Menorah, bereaved without

- 15 -
any lights in the house of the stranger. The holy vessels no
longer belonged to us, but we knew that they were safe and hidden.
And now the Menorah is to begin wandering again, and not home,
as we had thought, but it is being carried off, no one knows
where. But let us not weep. Weeping does not bring counsel.
Let us think."
The men listened mutely, their heads bowed. The old
man's hand still strayed over his beard, up and down. And still
as if he were consulting with himself, he said:
"The candelabra is of purified gold, and I have often
pondered why God wished our gift to be so costly; why did he
demand of Moses that the candelabra should be heavy in weight,
seven-branched, and ornamented with knops and flowers? I have
often thought whether this did not create danger for it, for evil
always comes of riches, and costly objects tempt robbers. But
again I recognise how vain is our thinking, and that what God
commands has a meaning far beyond our knowledge and our reason.
For I understand it now; only because they were costly our
sanctities been preserved through the ages. Had they been of
base metal and wrought without ornamentation, the robbers would
have broken them up unheedingly, and forged swords out of them
or chains. But as it is, they preserved the costly things for
being costly, without suspecting their sanctity. Thus one
robber takes them from the other, and none dares to destroy them,
and each of their wanderings leads them back to God.
"Now let us consider. The Barbarians, what do they know
of the sacred thing? That our candelabra is of gold is all that

- 16 -
they can see. If we could waken their greed, if we gave them
double or treble its weight in gold, we might perhaps succeed in
buying it. We cannot fight, we Jews; sacrifice alone is our
strength. We must send messengers to all the dispersed, in
every land, that they should help, all of us together, to redeem
the sacred thing. This year we must give double and treble as
our contribution to the Temple, the robe from our body, and the
ring off our finger. We must buy the holy vessel, even if it
cost seven times its weight in gold."
A sigh interrupted him. Hyrcanos Ben Hillel sadly lifted
his gaze.
"It is in vain. I have already attempted it," he said,
quietly. "It was my first thought, too. I went to their
valuers and scribes. But they were rude and harsh. I forced
my way into the presence of Genseric, and offered him a great
ransom. He listened sullenly, and shuffled his foot. I lost
all reason, I became insistent, and I boasted that the candelabra
had stood in Solomon's Temple, and that Titus had brought it
from Jerusalem as the glory of his triumph.
"Only then the Barbarian realised what he had gained, and
he laughed insolently: 'I have no need of your gold. I have
appropriated so much here that I can pave my horses' stables
with it, and stud their hoofs with gems. But if this cande-
labra is indeed Solomon's candelabra, then it is not for sale.
If Titus carried it before him in triumph to Rome, I shall have
it carried before me in triumph from Rome. If it has served

- 17 -
your God, it shall now serve the true God. Go!' - and he
ordered me out."
"You should not have gone away."
"I did not go. I flung myself down before him, I held
on to his knee. But his heart was harder than the iron bands
on his boots. He kicked me away from him, like a stone. And
then the slaves flogged me out. I barely escaped with my life."
Only now they knew why Hyrcanos Ben Hillel's clothes
were torn. Only now they saw the streak of blood on his fore-
head. Silently they sat there, and so still that they heard
in the distance the rattle of the carts, that kept rolling on
through the night, and now, too, echoing strangely from one end
of the city to the other, the muffled horns of the Vandals.
Then every sound was hushed. All had the same thought: The
great plundering is ended, the candelabra is lost!
Rabbi Eleazar looked up with an effort. "It is to-night,
you say, that they remove it?"
"To-night. They are taking it by cart along the Fortus
Street to the ships, and even while we talk, it is perhaps
being removed. Those horns were calling the rear-guard to-
gether. It will be put on board in the morning."
Rabbi Eleazar bowed his head still lower over the table.
It seemed that he had fallen asleep as he listened. He was
like one absent, and he was not aware that the others were
staring at him anxiously. Then suddenly he lifted his head,
and said calmly:

- 18 -
"To-night, you said. Good. Then we must go with it!"
They were all astounded. But the old man repeated it,
calmly and firmly.
"We must go with it. It is our duty. Remember the
Scripture and its command. When the Ark went forth, we depart-
ed. Only when it rested, did we rest. When God's sign
wanders, we must wander with it."
"But how cross the sea? We have no ships!"
"Then till the sea. It will take one night."
Hyrcanos stood up. "Rabbi Eleazar, as always, has
advised rightly. We must go with it. It is part of our
eternal road. When the Ark goes forth, and the Menorah, the
people must go with it, the whole community."
A low timorous voice came from the corner. Simche, the
carpenter, a hunchback, cried plaintively:
"But supposing they seize us? They have carried
hundreds already into slavery. They will beat us, they will
kill us, they will sell our children, and nothing will be gained
and nothing achieved."
"Silence!" cried another. "And eat your fear. If one
of us is taken, he is taken. If one of us dies, he dies for
the Sanctification. All of us must, and all of us will go!"
"Yes, all, all of us!" they cried in confused disorder.
But Eleazar, the Rabbi, signed to them to be silent.
He again closed his eyes, a habit of his when he wished to think.
Then he resolved:

- 19 -
"Simche is right. Do not revile him as a coward and a
weakling. He is right. We must not all risk our lives, and
go without purpose to meet the robbers in the night. For there
is nothing more sacred than life. God does not wish one single
life to be lost uselessly. Simche is right. They will seize
the young people, and make them slaves in their city. There-
fore the strong men and the young men must not go forth into the
night. But it is otherwise with us. We are old, and an aged
man is useless to anyone, and most of all to himself. We cannot
row in the galleys, we who have hardly enough strength to shovel
up the earth for our own grave, and even if death takes us he
does not gain much. We must go with the sacred vessels. Only
those who are over seventy will prepare for the road."
The old men, with their silver-white beards, stepped out
from the crowd. There were ten of them, and when Rabbi Eleazar,
the pure and clear, joined them, there were eleven. The
Patriarchs came to the minds of the younger men as they saw them
stand there, the last survivors of a bygone time, solemn and
grave. Once more the Rabbi turned, and stepped back among the
others.
"We will go, the old, the aged! Have no fear, you who
remain for our fate! Nevertheless, a child must go with us,
a boy, so that he may be a witness to testify to the next gene-
ration and the next. We shall soon die, our light is almost
spent, and soon our mouths will be dumb. But one must remain
for years and years to come, who has with his living gaze seen

- 20 -
the candelabra of the table of the Lord, so that the certainty
shall endure from generation to generation, that our most holy
vessel is not lost for ever, but only wanders further on its
eternal road. A child under age: and even if he does not
understand the meaning, he must go with us, to be a witness."
All were silent. Each thought anxiously of his own child,
of sending it out into night and danger. But Abtalion, the
master-dyer, had already risen.
"I go to bring Benjamin, my grandson. He is only seven,
as many years as there are branches to the candelabra, and this
seems to me to be a sign. Prepare yourselves meanwhile for the
road. Take for food whatever you find in my house. I go to
bring the child."
The old men seated themselves round the table, and the
younger brought them wine and food. But before they broke
bread, the Rabbi began the prayer that the forefathers at all
times repeated three times each day. And three times, the old
men repeated in their thin, broken voices, the yearning words:
"O Thou who art merciful, in Thine abundant mercy restore Thy
divine presence unto Zion, and the ordained service to Jerusalem."

THE BURIED CANDELABRA.

                                By Stefan Zweig.

     On a bright June day in the year 455, in the Circus
Maximus in Rome, just as a combat between two giant Heruli and
a pack of Hyrcanian boars had ended bloodily, about the third
hour of the afternoon, a growing uneasiness began to spread among
the thousands of spectators. At first, only those who were near
the detached State box, richly decorated with carpets and images,
in which the Emperor Maximus sat among his courtiers, noticed
that a messenger had entered it, covered with dust, and obviously
just dismounted after a wild ride, and that no sooner had the
Emperor heard what he had to say than, contrary to all custom,
he rose in the midst of the exciting games; the entire Court
followed him with the same conspicuous haste, and presently the
seats reserved for the Senators and other dignitaries had also
emptied. Such precipitate departure could not be without
weighty cause. In vain the fanfares announced a new combat,
the grating was lifted and a growling black-maned Nubian lion
was driven towards the short swords of the gladiators - a dark
wave of apprehension, frothing with the pale foam of questioning
and terrified faces, had surged up irresistibly and ran from tier
to tier. People sprang to their feet, pointed to the empty
seats of the patricians, questioned and clamoured, shouted and
whistled; then suddenly a confused rumour spread, without any-
one knowing who had started it, that the Vandals, the dreaded
pirates of the Mediterranean, had landed an immense fleet at
Portus, and were already on their way against the unsuspecting
